1: Rûḥî ḥubbĕlāh: 'my spirit is ruined/spent' — ḥābal (to bind, destroy, corrupt) in Pual: the spirit passively destroyed by forces beyond Job's control. Yāmay niʿăkû: 'my days are put out' — kāḥāh (to be extinguished, dimmed — of a lamp): life as a lamp going dark. Qĕbārôt lî: 'the grave is ready for me' — the plural of qeḇer as an intensive or honorific plural for the burial place.
2: ʾim-lōʾ hătûlîm ʿimmādî: 'surely there are mockers all around me' — hātûl (mocker, scoffer) from htl (to mock, deride) — a rare form. Bĕtamdĕrôtām (their provocation/their contentions): another rare term; the continuous hostile pressure of opponents.
3: Śîmāh-nāʾ ʿorbēnî ʿimmāk: 'Give me a pledge yourself' — the legal term ʿēreḇ (surety, guarantor): someone who stands in as financial or legal guarantee for another. Job asks God himself to be the ʿārēḇ (guarantor) for him — to post the bond that would allow Job's case to proceed. Mî hûʾ lĕyāḏî yitqāʿ: 'who else will strike hands with me?' — the handshake-pledge gesture of legal guarantee. No human will take the risk; only God can stand surety for Job before God.
4: Libbām ṣāpartā mibbiynāh: 'you have closed off their hearts from understanding' — the irony: God has prevented the very friends who claim wisdom from understanding Job's situation. Lōʾ tĕrōmmēm: 'you will not exalt them' — they have presumed to speak for God but God will not honor their presumption.
5: Lĕḥēleq yaggîḏ rēʿîm: 'he who informs against friends for personal gain' — ḥēleq (portion/share): the one who betrays associates for material advantage. ʿênê bānāyw tikhlenāh: 'his children's eyes will fail' — the punishment falls on the next generation; the betrayer's progeny will experience the blindness of deprivation.
6: Hissîgānî lĕmāšāl ʿammîm: 'he has turned me into a proverb among the nations' — māšāl (proverb, byword, taunt-saying): Job has become a negative example, a warning story. Topet-pānîm ʾehyeh: 'I am one before whose face men spit' — the extreme contempt gesture, used in laws of public shaming (Deut 25:9; Num 12:14).
7: Wattikhah mikkāʿas ʿênî: 'my eye has grown dim from sorrow' — kāʿas (vexation, grief) has physiologically dimmed his sight. ʿĂṣûmay kĕṣēl kullām: 'all my limbs are like shadows' — the wasting-away of the body; ṣēl (shadow) suggests insubstantiality, almost translucence.
8: Yāšōmmû yĕšārîm ʿal-zōʾt: 'upright men are appalled at this' — šmm (to be appalled, desolate) — the same root used for the desolation of Jerusalem. The yāšār (upright, straight) person is horrified not by Job's sin but by the sight of righteous suffering. Nāqî ʿal-ḥānēp yitʿōrār: 'the innocent rises up against the godless' — the moral reflex of the innocent community.
9: Wayyōʾḥez ṣaddîq darkô: 'the righteous holds to his way' — ṣaddîq (righteous/just) persisting in his path. Ûṭĕhar-yādayim: 'the one with clean hands' — the purity-term from Ps 24:4: clean-handed integrity as the qualification for the divine presence. Yaʾăṣōr kōaḥ: 'grows stronger' — the paradox of suffering that increases rather than depletes moral strength.
10: Wĕʾûlām kullām tāšubû ûbōʾû: 'but come, all of you, return and come' — Job's challenge to debate again. Lōʾ-ʾemṣāʾ bākem ḥākām: 'I cannot find a wise man among you' — the devastating reversal: the friends who came to impart wisdom are themselves found lacking it.
11: Yāmay ʿābārû: 'my days are over' — the perfect of completion: they are already done. Zimmōtay nittĕqû: 'my plans are torn apart' — zimmāh (plan, purpose, purpose) ripped away. Môrāšê lĕḇābî: 'the longings of my heart' — the deep possessions of the inner life, now lost.
12: Laylāh lĕyôm yāśîmû: 'they make night into day' — the perverse wisdom of those who reverse categories. Qārôb ʾôr mippĕnê-ḥōšeḵ: 'light is near, even in the darkness' — the friends' false optimism: offering easy resolution while darkness surrounds. A critique of cheap comfort.
13: ʾIm-ʾăqawweh šeʾôl bêtî: 'if I hope, Sheol is my home' — Sheol as the only dwelling Job can look forward to. Baḥōšeḵ rāpaʿdtî yĕṣûʿî: 'in the darkness I have spread my bed' — the language of habitation: Job is already at home in the realm of death. The qawwāh (hoping/waiting) that normally implies divine expectation is here redirected toward Sheol.
14: Laššaḥat qārāʾtî ʾābî ʾāttāh: 'I call corruption my father' — šaḥat (pit, grave, corruption) — the normal object of divine rescue (cf. Ps 16:10; Jon 2:7) is here addressed as family. Rimmāh ʾimmî waʾăḥōtî: 'the worm is my mother and my sister' — the darkest inversion of family language, establishing the intimacy of death.
15: ʾAyyēh ʾēpôh tiqwātî: 'Where then is my hope?' — tiqwāh (hope, cord — from qwh to wait/hope) is the key anthropological term for orientation toward the future. The double interrogative (where + where) drives home Job's complete loss of future-orientation. Wĕtiqwātî mî yĕšûrennāh: 'who can see my hope?' — no one can perceive it because it no longer exists visibly.
16: Baḏdê šeʾôl tērednāh: 'they will descend to the bars of Sheol' — baḏ (bar, plank — the structural bars of Sheol as a locked underworld). Hope and the one who held it descend together. Yaḥad ʿal-ʿāpār nāḥat: 'together we go down into the dust' — hope dies with the person. Or perhaps: hope is what will die first, and Job will follow. The ambiguity intensifies the desolation.
